title: Validating whether a new feature actually helps
scope: Tie-in to Ch 9. Explain that newly derived features must be tested against a baseline to ensure they provide actual predictive value, rather than just adding noise or causing overfitting.
key_concepts: Feature derivation is a hypothesis; validation is the proof. A new feature might logically make sense but fail to improve the model.
The baseline comparison: always evaluate the model's performance with and without the newly constructed feature.
Risk of overfitting: adding too many derived features can cause the model to memorize the training data. Cross-validation is essential.
Forward-pointer to Chapter 9: Detailed techniques like ablation studies and SHAP will be covered in depth in Chapter 9.

definitions.definition: A simple reference model used to measure if a new feature adds predictive value.
A validation technique to ensure the new feature's performance generalizes to unseen data.

examples_described: Compare a model using only raw age and income vs. a model using age, income, and a derived wealth-to-age ratio. If the validation metric doesn't improve, the derived feature should be discarded.
relations_to_other_chapters: Points forward to Chapter 9 (Feature Quality Evaluation) where ablation and feature importance are discussed in detail.
exclude: Do not explain SHAP or ablation studies in detail here; save it for Chapter 9.
Do not provide code for cross-validation.
